topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

The glossy school newsletter lay open on the kitchen table, the carefully printed photo of Mrs. Eleanor Vance staring back at me. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake that stole the air from my lungs. It was impossible. My son, Leo, was in the third grade at Northwood Elementary, and the photo was undeniably him. The teacher in the picture was Mrs. Vance. My hands began to tremble, the ceramic mug I was holding rattling against the saucer. I pushed myself up from the chair, my legs feeling unsteady, and walked to the window, staring out at the familiar street as if searching for an escape route that didn't exist. A cold dread coiled tight in my stomach, making my skin prickle. What did this mean?

[story 2]

I found the name on the school directory tucked inside Leo’s new backpack. It was Mrs. Vance. My heart started hammering against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat echoing in the quiet hallway. I reread the name, then looked down at Leo’s slightly scuffed shoes. It was the same face. A wave of nausea washed over me, making my vision swim momentarily. I backed away from the locker, bumping into a metal cabinet, and straightened up quickly, trying to steady the sudden rush of adrenaline. Every sound in the hallway seemed amplified—the squeak of sneakers, the distant chatter—and I felt a desperate need to flee the building entirely.

[story 3]

It happened while I was picking up Leo from after-school care. Mrs. Vance was standing by the gate, smiling warmly, her familiar cardigan a beacon in the late afternoon light. My carefully constructed composure shattered instantly. My jaw tightened so hard I could feel the strain in my temples. I managed a strangled, "Hello," my voice sounding thin and reedy. I stepped forward, my movements jerky and unnatural, unable to articulate anything coherent. My eyes darted from Mrs. Vance’s face to Leo’s bright, oblivious grin, a dizzying sense of wrongness flooding my senses. I needed to leave, needed distance, needing to figure out how to stop this impossible collision of memories.
